food_subgroup: Noodle soups and broth-based noodle dishes

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03311069 covers udon noodle dish as a category; no exact entry for udon noodle soup with broth. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers cooked wheat noodles as a category. No specific code for udon soup dish with broth and toppings. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for udon noodle soup as a prepared dish.

main_ingredients: optional toppings: tempura, fish cake (kamaboko), soft-boiled egg, tofu

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of udon noodle soup, approximately 400 g, comprising cooked udon noodles in dashi-soy broth with typical garnishes such as spring onions and fish cake.

nutrient_source.title: Udon noodle soup (kake udon) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite; cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Asian noodle soups
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central; FAO/INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry for a complete udon noodle soup dish. Values estimated from USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked udon noodles (FDC ID 168917, wheat noodles cooked), dashi broth components, and typical garnish proportions. Cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA noodle soup category data. Serving-size weighted composite per 100 g of finished dish (noodles ~40%, broth ~50%, toppings ~10%). Values represent a plain kake udon without heavy toppings.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; udon noodles entry
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: Udon noodles (white, boiled) have a published GI of approximately 55 (low) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). GL per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (55 × 58.0 g carbohydrate in 400 g serving) / 100 = 31.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from dashi broth and soy sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A single 400 g bowl of udon soup can contribute approximately 1280 mg sodium, representing over 55% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium broth, limit soy sauce additions, and avoid drinking the remaining broth.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 55) and high carbohydrate load from refined wheat udon noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic load of approximately 32 per standard bowl, udon noodle soup poses a moderate postprandial glucose challenge; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider smaller portions, pair with protein-rich toppings such as tofu or egg to blunt the glycemic response, and monitor blood glucose accordingly.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium from broth and low saturated fat content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While udon soup is low in saturated fat (approximately 0.4 g per 100 g), the high sodium content from dashi and soy sauce seasoning is a significant cardiovascular risk factor; patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, choose low-sodium broth variants, and avoid high-fat toppings such as tempura.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Japanese udon noodle soup (うどん) is a traditional Japanese dish consisting of thick, chewy wheat-flour noodles served in a clear, umami-rich dashi broth seasoned with soy sauce and mirin. The noodles are made from wheat flour, water, and salt, giving them a characteristic soft yet springy texture and mild flavour that absorbs the savoury broth. The broth is typically prepared from kombu (dried kelp) and katsuobushi (bonito flakes), producing a light golden stock with a delicate seafood-umami profile. Common toppings include sliced spring onions, fish cake (kamaboko), soft-boiled egg (onsen tamago), tempura pieces, or aburaage (fried tofu pouch). In Singapore, udon is widely available at Japanese restaurant chains and bento outlets, often served as a set meal. Nutritionally, udon soup is moderate in calories (approximately 75 kcal per 100 g), low in fat, and provides a moderate amount of carbohydrate from the noodles. The primary nutritional concern is sodium, which can be high due to the soy-sauce-seasoned broth. The glycemic index of udon noodles is approximately 55 (low), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Udon is not suitable for those with gluten intolerance or wheat allergy. It is generally dairy-free and can be adapted for vegetarian diets by substituting kombu-only dashi.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a bowl of udon?
What is the GI of udon noodles?
Is udon soup good for weight loss?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry for a complete udon noodle soup dish was available. Nutrient values were estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked wheat noodle data and dashi broth component data, weighted by typical serving composition. GI value is directly supported by the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled udon noodles. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach for macronutrients.
notes: Nutrient values represent a plain kake udon (noodles in broth with minimal toppings) at approximately 400 g per serving. Vitamin A (vitamin_a_mcg_rae) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA composite source for this dish. Vitamin B6 (vitamin_b6_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this composite. Vitamin B12 (vitamin_b12_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this composite. Folate (folate_mcg_dfe) is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this composite. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this composite. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this composite. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this composite. Trans fat is 0.0 (confirmed zero for plain wheat noodle and dashi broth preparation with no added hydrogenated fats). The input record listed the food as 'Japanese Udon/Ramen'; this record covers udon noodle soup specifically. Ramen is a distinct dish with different noodle composition (alkaline wheat noodles) and broth profile and should be processed as a separate record. The field survey field dataed (Shock Japanese Bento - Funan) serves both udon and ramen; this record addresses udon only. Halal status is not confirmed as dashi typically contains bonito (fish) and mirin may contain alcohol; marked is_halal false by default.
